title: Spinning angora rabbit fibre
subtitle: Working with a short-staple, moisture-sensitive down that drifts easily from the drafting zone
excerpt: Angora rabbit down has a staple length of only 2-5 cm and felts quickly if it gets damp during handling. Blending 20-30% angora into a merino base produces a stable yarn with the characteristic halo; pure angora is achievable on a wheel with a well-tensioned take-up. The short fibres become airborne during carding, so ventilate the room and damp-mop the floor before starting.
sourceNotes: Practical handling rules for angora (short-staple behaviour, felting risk, blending ratios) are standard across fibre guild resources and commercial angora suppliers' guidance. The 20-30% blend ratio for a stable wheel singles is widely cited in spinning guild workshops.

glossaryTerms.definition: The length of individual fibres in a lock or prepared top, measured in centimetres. Staple length determines which spinning techniques work: very short staples (under 5 cm) need a short drafting zone and fine control; long staples (over 12 cm) suit a long draw or worsted approach. Angora rabbit down sits at 2-5 cm, among the shortest commonly spun fibres.

The process of drawing fibres apart lengthways to thin them before twist enters the strand. The drafting zone is the section of fibre between the spinner's two hands; twist should not enter this zone until the fibres are thinned to the planned thickness.

The soft cloud of fibres that stand out from the surface of the finished yarn. Angora, kid mohair, and some brushed suri alpaca yarns are prized for their halo. A significant halo develops during wet-finishing and use as the short surface fibres fluff outward.

attrs.title: Wheel safety
attrs.body: Keep loose hair and clothing back from the drive band and flyer. The flyer turns fast; tie hair back and roll sleeves.

attrs.title: Airborne fibres
attrs.body: Angora fibres go airborne during carding. Ventilate the room and damp-mop the floor before starting.

content.text: Angora rabbit down is one of the more demanding spinning fibres. Its
staple length
is only 2-5 cm, so the
drafting

zone must stay very short. The smooth fibre surface has almost no tendency to grip itself or your fingers during preparation. Add to that its sensitivity to moisture. Body heat from damp hands compresses the fibres almost immediately, creating dense patches in the yarn. The reward is the
halo
. The finished yarn has a soft, luminous cloud of surface fibres. No other common fibre produces this quite as well.

For most wheel spinners, blending 20-30% angora into a prepared merino base is the practical starting point. The longer merino fibres hold the angora in place during drafting and reduce the risk of the strand drifting apart. Pure angora is achievable once you are comfortable with the handling, but it needs a well-tensioned take-up and an unfailingly dry touch.
What you need

items.name: Spinning wheel (castle or Saxony), set to a high ratio whorl

content.text: Preparing the blend

Weigh out your angora and merino in the planned ratio. For example: 20 g angora to 80 g merino for a 20% blend. Card them together in small portions on hand carders. Place a thin layer of merino on one card, add a dusting of angora across the surface, then add a second thin layer of merino on top. Transfer to the second carder and repeat two or three passes until the angora is evenly distributed.

Keep your hands dry throughout. If you are prone to damp hands, dust them lightly with fine cornflour before working with the fibre. Handle the carded rolags as little as possible before sitting at the wheel.
Spinning

Step 1: Set a short drafting zone. Because the staple length is 2-5 cm, your hands should be no more than 3-4 cm apart when drafting. This is noticeably shorter than the 6-10 cm zone suited to wool. If the hands drift further apart, the short angora fibres will simply slide out of the twist zone and the strand will thin unevenly.

Step 2: Set the wheel to a high ratio (12:1 or above) with moderate take-up. The short fibres need twist to reach them quickly before they drift. A slow-twisting wheel lets the drafting zone open before the fibres are secured.

Step 3: Use a controlled short forward draw. Treadle at a steady pace, draw forward no more than 3-4 cm, then allow the twist to consolidate before drawing again. Do not use a long draw with pure angora or a high-angora blend: the short fibres do not have enough length to hold themselves in the drafting zone against the tension of a long-draw extension.

Step 4: If spinning pure angora, keep the take-up moderately firm. A very loose take-up allows twist to accumulate in the yarn and cause the short fibres to bind together into hard sections. A moderate take-up pulls the twist evenly through the strand.
Finishing

Wind the singles onto a niddy-noddy and soak in cool water for 15 minutes. Angora felts quickly in warm water or under agitation: use cool, still water only. Press in a towel without wringing and hang to dry in a well-ventilated space. The halo develops fully as the yarn dries and continues to open slightly during the first wash of the finished project.
Common problems

items.problem: The strand drifts apart during drafting
items.cause: The drafting zone is too long for the 2-5 cm staple length, so the fibres slide free of the twist zone before they are secured.
items.fix: Bring your hands closer together: 3 cm apart maximum. If the problem persists, add another 5% merino to the blend to give the angora more fibre to grip against.

items.problem: Hard, matted patches appear in the singles
items.cause: Moisture from hands has partially felted the angora before the twist entered.
items.fix: Stop, let the yarn cool, and try to gently tease the matted section apart. Going forward, dust hands with cornflour and work in a cool room. Replace blended rolags that have been handled for more than a few minutes.

items.problem: Fibres become airborne and irritate the throat
items.cause: Angora fibres are very fine and go airborne easily. The carding stage produces the most fibre dust.
items.fix: Open windows, damp-mop the floor, and card near the open window so airflow carries fibres away. Take breaks if irritation develops. Some spinners wear a dust mask during the carding stage only.
